home.subtitle: on Nuclear Physics
home.aboutText1: Physez is an educational platform for studying complex sections of physics. Our goal is to make physics understandable and accessible through quality visualizations.
home.aboutText2: All video animations are created in Python using the Manim library and are completely free for students and teachers.

proton.name: Proton
proton.desc: A positively charged particle in the nucleus. The number of protons determines the atomic number of an element.
proton.discovery: Ernest Rutherford, 1919 — experiments on splitting nitrogen nuclei with alpha particles.

neutron.name: Neutron
neutron.desc: A neutral particle in the nucleus. Neutrons add mass to the atom and affect nuclear stability.
neutron.discovery: James Chadwick, 1932 — bombardment of beryllium with alpha particles, detection of uncharged radiation.

electron.name: Electron
electron.desc: A negatively charged particle moving around the nucleus. Electrons determine chemical properties.
electron.discovery: Joseph Thomson, 1897 — experiments with cathode rays, deflection in electric and magnetic fields.

quiz.title: Final Test on
quiz.codeHint: Leave empty if you haven't taken the pre-test
quiz.name: Name
quiz.question: Question
quiz.answerAll: Please answer all questions before finishing
quiz.anonymity: Testing is anonymous. You can enter any name — this data is only needed for your teacher's convenience. Results will be used in research project statistics. After the study is complete, all data will be deleted and never shared with third parties.

survey.title: Survey for
survey.intro: Please answer questions about the animations. Your opinion is very important to us!
survey.thanksMessage: Your answers will help us improve the quality of educational materials.

preQuiz.title: Pre-Test on
preQuiz.description: This test will help assess your knowledge before studying the material.

rutherford.title: Rutherford Experiment
rutherford.description: In 1911, Ernest Rutherford conducted his famous alpha particle scattering experiment on gold foil. The results showed that an atom has a tiny massive nucleus, disproving Thomson's 'plum pudding' model. This experiment laid the foundation for nuclear physics and the planetary model of the atom.

alpha.title: Alpha Decay
alpha.description: Alpha decay is a process in which a nucleus emits an alpha particle (helium-4 nucleus). The phenomenon was discovered by Becquerel in 1896 and explained by Gamow in 1928 through quantum tunneling. Alpha particles have high ionizing ability but low penetrating power.

beta.title: Beta Decay
beta.description: In beta decay, a neutron in the nucleus transforms into a proton, emitting an electron and an antineutrino (or vice versa in β⁺ decay). Pauli predicted the neutrino in 1930 to explain the continuous energy spectrum. Fermi developed the theory of beta decay in 1934, linking it to weak interaction.

gamma.title: Gamma Radiation
gamma.description: Gamma radiation is high-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted by a nucleus when transitioning from an excited state to the ground state. It was discovered by Paul Villard in 1900. Gamma rays have the highest penetrating power among all types of radioactive radiation and are used in medicine and industry.

halflife.title: Half-Life
halflife.description: Half-life is the time it takes for half of the radioactive atoms in a sample to decay. It is a fundamental characteristic of each radioactive isotope, independent of external conditions. The law of radioactive decay, discovered by Rutherford and Soddy, describes the exponential decrease in the number of undecayed nuclei.

interactions.title: Nuclear Interactions
interactions.description: Nuclear (strong) interactions hold nucleons together inside the nucleus, overcoming the electrostatic repulsion of protons. Yukawa predicted the existence of mesons as carriers of nuclear forces in 1935. The strong interaction is the most powerful of the four fundamental forces but only acts at distances of about 10⁻¹⁵ m.

droplet.title: Liquid Drop Model
droplet.description: The liquid drop model, proposed by Gamow and developed by Bohr and Wheeler in the 1930s, describes the atomic nucleus by analogy with a drop of charged liquid. It successfully explains the binding energy of nucleons and underlies the Weizsäcker formula. This model became key to understanding the mechanism of heavy nucleus fission.

decay.title: Nuclear Fission
decay.description: The fission of a heavy nucleus into two fragments was discovered by Hahn and Strassmann in 1938 and explained by Meitner and Frisch. The fission of uranium-235 releases enormous energy — about 200 MeV per fission event. This discovery led to the creation of nuclear reactors and changed the course of world history.

chain.title: Chain Reaction
chain.description: A nuclear chain reaction occurs when neutrons from one fission event cause the fission of other nuclei. Fermi achieved the first controlled chain reaction on December 2, 1942, in Chicago. Controlling chain reactions is the basis of all nuclear reactors and the key to peaceful use of atomic energy.

footer.about: Educational platform for studying nuclear physics
footer.author: Project Author
